**\#hackthekitty 2026**

# **Project Report**

**Project Name: Cat-A-Log**

**Reference ID: 3E01II47**

# 1\. Executive Summary

_In 2-3 sentences, summarize what your project does and the impact or value it delivers. Write this last, once everything else is done._

# 2\. Project Overview

## 2a. Why you're building what you're building

Cats and outdoor pets that roam freely are all over the neighborhood, but nobody has a shared way to track them. Someone sees the same cat outside their building every day and has no idea if it's fixed, fed, or in trouble. Volunteers doing TNR work end up re-documenting animals other volunteers already logged, just because there's no shared record. And if a cat disappears or gets hurt, there's nothing to check against.

Cat-A-Log is a crowdsourced registry. Anyone can photograph and geolocate a cat they run into, and it goes into a shared, map-based catalog for the neighborhood. CLIP embeddings flag when a newly tagged cat looks like one already in the database, catching duplicates and tying separate sightings back to the same animal.

Right now there's no lightweight, public tool for a community to track its own cats collectively, whether that's strays needing TNR, community cats someone's feeding, or a pet that keeps turning up three blocks from home.

## 2b. How it relates to the theme

Cat-A-Log combines community participation with AI to turn everyday sightings into one structured record. Anyone can log a cat while out on a walk, no special effort required. Multimodal embeddings compare photos by visual similarity, so if a cat's already registered, the app can tell whoever spots it next. Individual sightings add up into a map of the neighborhood's cats, visible to everyone, not just the person who tagged it.

That's the whole idea: let AI do the tedious photo-matching, and let people handle the rest.

## 2c. Target Audience

- **Residents** who see cats around the neighborhood and want to know, or share, who they are, where they've been seen, and whether they need help.
- **Caretakers** and **feeders** running informal colonies who want to track which cats show up at their feeding spot.
- **TNR** **volunteers** and **welfare** **groups** who need to know how many cats are in an area, how many are unneutered, and who needs medical attention, so they know where to put their time.
- And **owners** whose cats roam outdoors, who get real value out of a stranger's photo showing where their cat's been wandering.

It's a mobile-first PWA because the core interaction, spot a cat, snap a photo, tag the location, happens standing on a sidewalk, not sitting at a desk.

# 3\. Key Features

- **Interactive Cat Map**

Full-screen map showing all registered cats as pins with their photos. Users can pan and search a new area, filter by ear-tipped status or welfare tags, search cats by name, and tap a pin to preview the cat's details. A "locate me" button centers the map on the user's current position.

- **Photo-First Tagging Flow**

A guided multi-step flow for registering a cat: capture or select a photo, auto-detect GPS location, review AI-suggested matches, name the cat, add details (ear-tipped, welfare tags, notes). It's built for on-the-go use and stores progress locally in IndexedDB until the upload completes.

- **AI-Powered Duplicate Detection**

When a user tags a new cat, the app finds nearby cats by GPS radius and uses Voyage AI's multimodal CLIP embeddings to rank how visually similar they are to the new photo. If it finds a match, the user can confirm it's the same cat and add a new sighting instead of creating a duplicate entry.

- **Shareable Catch Cards**

After tagging a cat, the app generates a "catch card" image server-side (via \`next/og\`) and shows it to the user. Cards include the cat's photo, name, and a tier based on sighting count. Users can share the card directly through the Web Share API or download it as a PNG.

- **User Profiles**

Each user has a public profile page showing their avatar, bio, list of tagged cats, and a featured cat. Profile owners can upload and crop an avatar, edit their bio, set a featured cat, and sign out. Profiles also generate shareable OG image cards for social previews.

- **Google OAuth Authentication**

Users sign up and log in with Google. After first signup, they complete a profile setup (username and bio). The proxy guards authenticated routes and redirects accordingly.

- **Social Sharing & Link Previews**

Every cat and profile has a dedicated URL with full Open Graph and Twitter Card metadata, so shared links generate custom preview images on social platforms.

# 4\. Technology Stack

| Layer                | Technology                                      |
| :------------------- | :---------------------------------------------- |
| Language             | TypeScript5                                     |
| Framework            | Next.js 16 (App Router)                         |
| Frontend Library     | React 19                                        |
| Styling              | Tailwind CSS 4                                  |
| UI Components        | shadcn/ui, Base UI (\`@base-ui/react\`)         |
| Icons                | Lucide React                                    |
| Forms & Validation   | React Hook Form, Zod                            |
| Maps                 | Leaflet, React Leaflet                          |
| Map Clustering       | Supercluster                                    |
| Image Cropping       | react-image-crop                                |
| Theming              | next-themes                                     |
| Toasts/Notifications | Sonner                                          |
| Authentication       | Supabase Auth (Google OAuth)                    |
| Database             | Supabase (PostgreSQL \+ pgvector)               |
| Storage              | Supabase Storage (photo uploads)                |
| AI / Embeddings      | Voyage AI (voyage-multimodal-3 CLIP embeddings) |
| Error Monitoring     | Sentry (\`@sentry/nextjs\`)                     |
| PWA                  | Custom Service Worker, Web App Manifest         |
| Testing              | Vitest, Testing Library, Playwright (E2E)       |
| CI/CD                | GitHub Actions                                  |
| Linting & Formatting | ESLint, Prettier, Husky, lint-staged            |
| Bundle Analysis      | @next/bundle-analyzer, Lighthouse CI            |
| Platform             | Vercel (hosting), Supabase (BaaS)               |

#

#

#

#

#

#

#

#

# 5\. Technical Architecture

_![][image1]_

**_Client Layer (User's Device)_**

_\- **Map Page:** Renders an interactive Leaflet map with clustered cat pins. Fetches nearby cats via Supabase's nearby_cats SQL function based on the visible map bounds._

_\- **Tag Flow:** Multi-step wizard for registering a cat. Captures a photo, reads GPS coordinates, and stores the pending tag in IndexedDB before uploading._

_\- **Profile Page:** Displays user stats, their tagged cats, and a featured cat. Supports avatar upload with cropping._

**_Server Layer (Vercel)_**

_\- **proxy.ts:** Intercepts every request. Refreshes the Supabase session cookie and redirects unauthenticated users away from protected routes._

_\- **API Routes:** Server-side endpoints that handle sensitive operations:_

    *\- /api/catch-cat: Inserts a new cat record and triggers embedding generation.*

    *\- /api/match: Accepts a photo, generates its CLIP embedding via Voyage AI, and calls nearby\_cats\_by\_similarity to rank candidate cats by visual similarity.*

    *\- /api/catch-card and /api/profile-card: Generate shareable OG images server-side using next/og (Satori).*

_\-_ **Server Components:** _Fetch data directly from Supabase at request time (profiles, cat metadata) and render HTML with full SEO/OG metadata._

**_Data Layer (Supabase)_**

_\- **Auth:** Manages user sessions, Google OAuth flow, and issues JWTs consumed by the Supabase client libraries._

_\- **PostgreSQL \+ pgvector**: Stores all application data. The pgvector extension enables cosine-similarity search over CLIP embeddings for visual cat matching._

_\- **Storage:** Object storage for cat photos and user avatars, with public URL access._

**_AI Layer (Voyage AI)_**

_\- Called server-side only. Converts a cat photo into a 1024-dimensional CLIP embedding stored in the cats.photo_embedding column and used for similarity ranking._

**_Data Flow: Tagging a New Cat_**

_1\. User takes photo, GPS auto-detected_

_2\. App queries nearby_cats(lat, lng, radius) — Supabase returns candidates_

_3\. App sends photo \+ candidate IDs to /api/match_

_4\. Server generates embedding via Voyage AI, calls nearby_cats_by_similarity, returns ranked matches_

_5\. User confirms "not a match" — enters name \+ details_

_6\. Pending tag saved to IndexedDB (offline-safe)_

_7\. Flush page uploads photo to Supabase Storage, inserts cat row via /api/catch-cat_

_8\. Server stores embedding in cats.photo_embedding for future matching_

_9\. User sees catch card, can share via Web Share API_

# 6\. Testing Matrix

_Optional section, but this will improve your marks on technical execution. List the manual test cases you ran to verify your project works as intended._

| Feature / Flow                      | Steps                                                                                                                                                                                         | Expected Result                                                                               | Actual Result                                                                          | Pass / Fail |
| :---------------------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :---------- |
| Google OAuth Registration           | 1\. Navigate to /register 2\. Click "Sign up with Google" 3\. Complete Google consent screen 4\. Redirected to /setup-profile 5\. Enter username and bio 6\. Submit the form                  | User account created, profile row inserted, redirected to /map                                | Account created, profile saved, landed on /map with bottom nav visible                 | Pass        |
| Login with existing account         | 1\. Navigate to /login 2\. Click "Continue with Google" 3\. Select existing Google account                                                                                                    | Authenticated and redirected to /map (skipping setup-profile since profile exists)            | Redirected to /map, session active, map loads                                          | Pass        |
| Auth guard (unauthenticated access) | 1\. Clear cookies / use incognito 2\. Navigate directly to /map                                                                                                                               | Redirected to /login page                                                                     | Redirected to /login with return_to parameter preserved                                | Pass        |
| Tag a new cat (full flow)           | 1\. Tap Tag FAB in bottom nav 2\. Take or upload a photo 3\. Confirm GPS location on map 4\. Review candidate matches 5\. Enter cat name 6\. Set ear-tipped, add notes 7\. Confirm submission | Photo uploaded, cat record created in database, redirected to catch card completion screen    | Progress bar shows upload and registration stages, cat appears on map after completion | Pass        |
| Confirm match (add sighting)        | 1\. Select candidate cat as match 2\. Tap "That's them\!"                                                                                                                                     | New sighting added to existing cat, no duplicate created, sighting count incremented          | Sighting added, redirected to completion screen showing matched cat                    | Pass        |
| Map displays cats with clustering   | 1\. Navigate to /map 2\. Allow location permission 3\. Observe pins 4\. Zoom out (clusters) 5\. Zoom in (individual pins)                                                                     | Pins at correct GPS, cluster when zoomed out, expand when zoomed in                           | Pins correct, clusters show count badge, clicking cluster zooms to expand              | Pass        |
| Map search and filter               | 1\. Type cat name in search bar 2\. Select a result 3\. Open filter sheet 4\. Toggle "ear-tipped only"                                                                                        | Search flies map to selected cat; filter hides non-matching pins                              | Map flies to cat, pin highlighted; filter removes non-ear-tipped cats from view        | Pass        |
| Cat preview card on map             | 1\. On /map, tap a cat pin                                                                                                                                                                    | Preview card slides up showing photo, name, distance, sighting count, welfare tags            | Card appears with correct data, photo loads, tapping navigates to share view           | Pass        |
| Profile page (own profile)          | 1\. Navigate to /profile/me 2\. View avatar, username, bio, cats list 3\. Tap avatar to upload new one                                                                                        | Displays stats, list of tagged cats, avatar upload dialog works with crop                     | All data correct, avatar crop works, new avatar reflects immediately                   | Pass        |
| Share catch card                    | 1\. After tagging, arrive at completion screen 2\. Tap "Share this catch" 3\. Observe generated card image                                                                                    | Server-generated card renders; Web Share API opens native share (or downloads PNG on desktop) | Card loads with tier styling, share sheet opens on mobile, PNG downloads on desktop    | Pass        |
| Dark mode                           | 1\. Open profile page 2\. Toggle theme 3\. Navigate across pages                                                                                                                              | All pages respect theme, proper contrast, map tiles remain visible                            | Theme persists across navigation, correct contrast in both modes                       | Pass        |

# 8\. Future Improvements

- **_Community match voting_**

_Duplicate detection today is AI-assisted, but one person makes the final call. We'd rather make it a community decision. A user proposes that two records are the same cat, others vote confirm or deny, and the system resolves it once enough votes come in. No moderators needed. The database tables for this already exist; what's missing is the UI._

- **_Gamification_**

_The tier system on catch cards would grow into something bigger: badges when you hit milestones (first cat tagged, ten sightings, first confirmed match), streak rewards for logging daily, and a neighborhood leaderboard. People like seeing their name on a list. We'd lean into that._

- **_Leaderboard_**

_Neighborhood and city-wide rankings by cats tagged, sightings logged, and matches confirmed. Less about competition, more about making regular contributors visible to each other._

- **_Weekly "find this cat" events_**

_Each week, the app picks a cat that hasn't been sighted recently in a local area and asks people to look for it. Log a fresh sighting and you get bonus recognition. It doubles as a welfare check for cats that might have moved or gone missing._

- **_Better notifications and coordination_**

  - _\- Get notified when someone else spots a cat you tagged_

  - _\- Welfare alerts for cats flagged as needing medical attention near you_

  - _\- Feeding coordination: mark when you've fed a community cat so nobody doubles up_

  - _\- Collar detection in photos to help reunite lost pets with owners_

- **_Richer cat profiles_**

_A sighting timeline. A mini-map of everywhere the cat's been spotted. Temperament notes from multiple people. A photo gallery that shows how the cat looks over time. Right now profiles are thin; these additions would make each cat feel like a real, tracked individual._

- **_Native app_**

_The PWA works, but a native app unlocks things the browser can't do well. Background location for passive sighting detection. Reliable push notifications that don't depend on the browser staying open. Direct camera access with faster shot-to-upload. Offline mode that queues sightings when you're in a park with no signal and syncs when you're back. App Store and Play Store presence also helps with trust and discoverability, which matters for a community tool that needs a critical mass of users to be useful._

# 9\. Tools You Used

_List any tools that helped you build the project (e.g. AI coding assistants, design tools, recording software, etc.)_

- Claude

- Kiro

- Figma

- OBS

- Remotion

# 11\. Learnings & Takeaways

_Most of what I learned on this project was about working with AI agents, not about the stack itself. The technical side (Next.js, Supabase, Tailwind) was fairly standard. What wasn't standard was figuring out how to prompt an agent so it produces something usable on the first pass, without three rounds of corrections._

_The biggest shift was learning to front-load context. Vague instructions get vague code. Specific instructions with clear constraints, examples, and explicit "don't do this" guardrails get output you can actually ship. By the end I was spending more time writing the prompt than I would have spent writing the code manually, but the results were consistent and I could move faster across the whole project because of it._

_I also learned that agentic workflows reward structure. The more I formalized my process (skills files, steering docs, agent context), the less time I spent fixing drift or re-explaining things mid-session. It's a different kind of engineering: you're designing the process that produces the code, not just the code itself._
